g.data-narration: Welcome to Introduction to Database Management Systems. This module covers fundamental concepts essential for every CSE student.
A Database Management System is software that enables users to define, create, maintain, and control access to databases efficiently.
Data is raw, unprocessed facts. Information is processed, meaningful data that provides value and context to users.
Traditional file processing systems suffer from data redundancy, inconsistency, isolation, and security problems.
g.text: Need for DBMS - Problems of File Systems

g.data-narration: DBMS provides data abstraction, data independence, efficient access, integrity, security, concurrency control, and recovery mechanisms.
Four types of database users: DBAs manage the system, designers create structure, developers build applications, and end users interact with data.
ANSI/SPARC has three levels: External for user views, Conceptual for logical structure, and Internal for physical storage.
Physical data independence allows storage changes without affecting logical schema. Logical independence allows schema changes without affecting applications.
g.text: Change storage without affecting logical schema

g.data-narration: Based on number of users: Single-user DBMS serves one user, while multi-user DBMS supports multiple concurrent users.
g.text: Types of DBMS - By Number of Users

g.data-narration: By distribution: Centralized databases store data in one location. Distributed databases store data across multiple locations connected via network.
Five major data models: Hierarchical (tree), Network (graph), Relational (tables), ER (entities/relationships), and Object-Oriented.
The relational model organizes data in tables with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes). SQL is the standard language for relational databases.
In file systems, updating one file leads to inconsistency. DBMS ensures all related records reflect changes automatically through referential integrity.

ACID properties ensure reliable transactions: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state to valid state), Isolation (concurrent appears sequential), Durability (committed data is permanent).
Key takeaways: DBMS evolved to solve file system problems. It provides abstraction, independence, security, concurrency control, and recovery mechanisms.
g.text: • DBMS emerged to overcome file processing limitations
• Three-level ANSI/SPARC architecture: External, Conceptual, Internal
• Relational model is most widely used in modern databases
• ACID properties ensure reliable transaction processing

g.data-narration: For exams: Know difference between data and information, memorize file system problems, draw ANSI/SPARC architecture, understand data independence, and practice ACID properties.
